column1: What is MS SQL Server?
column2: Microsoft's proprietary relational database management system

column1: What are popular tools used to interact with MS SQL servers?
column2: PowerUpSQL, SQLRecon, SQL-BOF, sqlcmd, HeidiSQL, and SSMS

column1: How can SQL server instances be enumerated using Kerberos?
column2: By querying for SPNs (Service Principal Names) used to support Kerberos authentication, which reveals hostnames and service accounts

column1: What information can SPN enumeration reveal about SQL servers?
column2: The hostname of the SQL servers and the service accounts used to run them

column1: What is an alternative method to enumerate SQL servers when Kerberos is not configured?
column2: Scanning for open ports known to be used by MS SQL Server

column1: What is the default port used by MS SQL Server?
column2: Port 1433 (TCP), with SQLBrowser using 1434 UDP

column1: What service must be running to collect minimal SQL server information without any role?
column2: The SQLBrowser service on port 1434/UDP

column1: What minimal information can be collected via SQLBrowser without any SQL role?
column2: Server name, instance name, and version

column1: What is the minimum SQL role required for any further interaction beyond SQLBrowser info?
column2: The public role

column1: What additional information does the public role allow you to collect?
column2: The SQL server process's PID and other server details

column1: What roles are of particular interest when enumerating SQL permissions?
column2: serveradmin, securityadmin, and sysadmin

column1: What does the public role allow beyond basic server info collection?
column2: Permission to query the database instance, useful for data-hunting

column1: What scenario allows code execution via SQL without local admin privileges?
column2: Having a privileged SQL role such as sysadmin without local admin on the server itself

column1: Are xp_cmdshell, OLE Automation, and SQL CLR enabled by default?
column2: No, they are all disabled by default on new SQL server installations, but can be enabled by a sysadmin

column1: What is xp_cmdshell?
column2: A stored procedure that spawns a command shell, passes a string for execution, and returns output as rows of text

column1: What is a key limitation of the xp_cmdshell command string?
column2: It cannot contain more than one set of double quotation marks; a single pair is required if spaces are present

column1: What user context are xp_cmdshell commands executed under?
column2: The context of the principal running the sqlservr process, typically SYSTEM or a service account

column1: Which SQL code execution technique is the only one that can return output?
column2: xp_cmdshell

column1: What should you do after enabling a procedure like xp_cmdshell?
column2: Disable it again afterwards to return it to its original state

column1: What is OLE Automation in the context of SQL Server?
column2: A technology allowing SQL Server to interact with arbitrary COM objects via stored procedures

column1: What is OLE Automation's original design purpose?
column2: To allow one application to link objects into another, originally designed for Microsoft Office (e.g., embedding Excel sheets into Word documents)

column1: What technology did OLE eventually become the foundation for?
column2: The Component Object Model (COM)

column1: What are the OLE Automation stored procedures available in SQL Server?
column2: sp_OACreate, sp_OADestroy, sp_OAGetErrorInfo, sp_OAGetProperty, sp_OAMethod, sp_OASetProperty, sp_OAStop

column1: What are some COM objects that OLE Automation can abuse for code execution?
column2: ScriptControl (for arbitrary JS or VBS) and WbemScripting (for WMI process spawning)

column1: What is the key limitation of OLE Automation compared to xp_cmdshell?
column2: OLE Automation cannot return output, so it is limited to executing one-liners

column1: Why is a reverse port forward often needed when exploiting SQL servers with OLE?
column2: Servers are typically restricted from sending traffic outside the network boundary

column1: What is SQL Common Language Runtime (CLR)?
column2: A feature introduced in SQL Server 2005 that integrates the .NET Framework, allowing stored procedures to be written in managed .NET languages

column1: What .NET languages can be used to write SQL CLR stored procedures?
column2: Any managed .NET language, such as C# and VB.NET

column1: What class and attribute are required for a C# method to be compatible with SQL CLR?
column2: It must be in a partial class called StoredProcedures and decorated with the SqlProcedureAttribute

column1: What can SQL CLR stored procedures be used for in an attack?
column2: Running PowerShell one-liners, shellcode injection via P/Invoke, or any arbitrary C# code execution

column1: What are SQL server links?
column2: Connections between a SQL instance and additional data sources, including other SQL servers

column1: What security context do you receive on a linked server?
column2: It depends on how the link is configured — it may use hardcoded local credentials, hardcoded domain credentials, or inherit the current session's security context

column1: What is the significance of a link configured with the local sa account?
column2: You will have sysadmin privileges on the linked server, even if you don't have that access on the initial server

column1: What is RPC Out and why is it important for linked server attacks?
column2: RPC Out is required to call stored procedures on a linked server; without it, you cannot enable features like xp_cmdshell or CLR on the linked server

column1: What is SeImpersonatePrivilege?
column2: A token privilege often held by service accounts that can be abused for local privilege escalation

column1: What types of service accounts commonly have SeImpersonatePrivilege?
column2: Accounts running services such as MS SQL Server and Internet Information Services (IIS)

column1: What is a local virtual account in the context of SQL Server service accounts?
column2: An account type that can access network resources using the computer account's credentials, but has limited local permissions and is not a local admin

column1: What is SweetPotato?
column2: A tool that exploits SeImpersonatePrivilege by creating a named pipe, coercing a SYSTEM process to connect to it, impersonating its access token, and spawning an arbitrary process as SYSTEM

column1: How does SweetPotato achieve privilege escalation?
column2: It creates a named pipe, coerces a SYSTEM process into connecting to it, impersonates that process's access token, and uses it to spawn another process as SYSTEM

column1: What does SweetPotato ultimately require to execute its privilege escalation?
column2: A program (payload) that exists on disk to be executed as SYSTEM
